title: Policy as Code: Enforcing Guardrails Automatically
description:
## Block Non-Compliant Infrastructure Automatically

Governance has a growing list of hard rules: no public S3 buckets, no privileged pods, all container images must be signed, and all resources must carry a `cost-center` tag. Today these are enforced by humans reading Terraform plans and Kubernetes manifests during review, which is slow and error-prone.

You want to enforce these rules **automatically and consistently** — failing a Terraform plan in CI and rejecting a non-compliant Kubernetes manifest at the cluster — without relying on a reviewer to catch violations.

Which approach is the right fit?

options.text: Adopt policy as code: express the rules as machine-evaluated policies (e.g. OPA/Rego) and enforce them at the gates — evaluate the Terraform plan in CI (e.g. `conftest`/OPA) and run an admission controller (e.g. OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno) that rejects non-compliant Kubernetes resources at admission.
Write a detailed governance wiki page and a PR checklist template; require reviewers to tick each rule before approving, which standardizes enforcement without new tooling.
Add a nightly cron job that scans the live cloud account and cluster, files a ticket for each violation found, and emails the owning team to remediate within the SLA.
Enable cloud provider config rules only (e.g. AWS Config) so violations are recorded for audit; since auditing creates accountability, teams will stop creating non-compliant resources.

explanation: Policy as code expresses governance rules as version-controlled, machine-evaluated code and enforces them programmatically at the points where infrastructure is defined or admitted. OPA with the Rego language is the canonical engine: in CI you evaluate the Terraform plan JSON (e.g. via conftest/OPA) and fail the pipeline on a violation, and in the cluster an admission controller such as OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno intercepts API requests and denies non-compliant resources (privileged pods, unsigned images, missing tags) before they're persisted. This is automatic, consistent, and shifts enforcement left while still backstopping at admission. (b) still depends entirely on a human remembering and correctly applying the checklist — exactly the slow, error-prone process you're replacing. (c) and (d) are detective controls: they find violations after the fact (the bad bucket already exists, possibly exposed for hours) and rely on follow-up remediation; they do not prevent the non-compliant resource from being created. Preventive policy-as-code gates block the violation at the gate, before it takes effect.

hints.title: Preventive vs detective
hints.body: Scanning after the fact files tickets; a policy gate refuses the bad change before it's applied. The requirement is to block, not to report.

hints.title: Two enforcement points
hints.body: Evaluate the Terraform plan in CI (OPA/conftest) and reject manifests at the Kubernetes admission stage (Gatekeeper/Kyverno) — same policies, both gates.
